text: The article is grounded within a “lived religion” perspective of practical theology. With institutional religion quickly becoming less relevant in modern life, people are searching for meaningful experiences within social and cultural contexts. Bringing a lived theology perspective to bear within the narrative of the Harry Potter series this article illuminates the religious potential of popular culture that enables a space to emerge where teenagers can experience new dimensions of spiritual development which impacts their religious identity formation. Against the background of a culture that has given itself over to consumerist ideology, in which everyday life has become mundane and devoid of transcendence, the Harry Potter series functions as a “sacred space” within which teenagers experience the gospel of Christianity through the narrative vehicle of sacrificial love, as well as communal fellowship, both of which enable an embodied engagement with evangelical values in ways that affirms adolescent life worlds.
